title: Strong culture, impactful work, and great learning environment
body: Strong culture, impactful work, and great learning environment
pros: Fidelity offers a strong culture of integrity, collaboration, and continuous learning. Leadership is supportive and encourages data-driven decision-making. The company provides exposure to large-scale financial datasets and meaningful analytical projects that directly impact business outcomes. There are good growth opportunities, competitive benefits, and a stable work environment. Teams are generally collaborative, and cross-functional communication is encouraged.
cons: Some processes can be structured and slow due to compliance and regulatory requirements. Decision-making may involve multiple approvals, which can delay implementation. Workload can increase during month-end or quarter-end reporting cycles. Certain legacy systems may require modernization to improve efficiency.

title: Corporate Machine
pros: Benefits are great, but quickly turn into golden handcuffs where âadvisorsâ are 6 weeks from the next bonus. Comp is market adjustments where everyone gets paid the same for the same role regardless of workload or merit. Great place to start in the industry, get CFP or other designations and build experience and then you can jump to whenever you want after 3-5 years of grinding it out here.
cons: Completely sales focused, they say they are âhelping peopleâ, but really the focus and pay structure is to push clients into products as fast as possible with the minimal amount of planning needed. If you are serious about financial planning, go to an RIA. If you want to make a lot of money, and have managers always want more (youâre never doing enough), then this is a good fit. Everyone gets paid the same in a role unless your manager likes you (favoritism), then youâll get shares which payout 3 years later. Constant stack ranking and comparisons vs peers. âWhat have you done for me this monthâ mindset. The year I passed the CFP and significantly grew skills/performance, I received a 0% raise and inflation was 6%. The next year was 1.4%, and the year after that was 3%. Over 3 years I lost almost 15% of my pay to inflation while being the most highly educated and certified person in my branch.

body: Amazon Politics And Culture, But Without The Pay
pros: The day-to-day project workload is well-paced, and the deadlines are realistic, which is a rarity in many tech firms. The office facilities are excellent and provide a great working environment. Professionally, the role offers invaluable experience in managing massive data volumes under high regulatory pressure, teaching you how to build robust systems where there is very little room for error. The base salary is relatively modest, but when you factor in all of the bonuses, retirement plan matching, etc, the pay package is substantial, especially relative to the local cost of living.

Is 8 am on time? 9 am? is 4 pm the end of the day? 5 pm? Translation: Track onsite attendance arbitrarily in order to penalize people arbitrarily despite actual work performance.

title: Great place to work!
pros: Fidelity offers a strong culture centered on client outcomes, professional development, and ethical standards. The firm invests heavily in training, licensing support, and ongoing education, which makes it an excellent place to build a long-term career in financial services. Leadership is generally supportive, and the resources available to serve clientsâtechnology, research, and internal partnersâare among the best in the industry. Compensation and benefits are competitive, and work-life balance is better than many comparable firms, especially for a client-facing role.
cons: Like most large organizations, Fidelity can feel bureaucratic at times, and decision-making may move slowly due to layers of approval. Performance expectations are high, particularly around metrics and compliance, which can feel rigid depending on your management team. Advancement opportunities can be competitive, and internal mobility often requires patience and persistence.

title: Terrible culture with more terrible policies.
body: Terrible culture with more terrible policies.
pros: I donât know how Fidelity continues to get high rankings for workplace. The pros are the facility, lunch, and resources that are available for you to learn outside your role via seminars and guest speakers. Thatâs only if your business unit can or will allow you the opportunity to step away from your responsibilities to attend.
cons: Everything else, a toxic culture that they label as networking. Movement at the company is all about who you know not what you contribute or have experience doing in the past. There are no policies that actually exit because each business unit makes their own. This means when you reach out to HR they just point you right back to your business unit. Promotions are given and not posted for applicants leading to blatant favoritism under the guise of âthey networkedâ

cons: Complete disaster from a management perspective, in the 3 years I have been with the company I have had 8 different managers all with different expectations and ways of doing things which makes it impossible to get good footing with anything, PTO is available but often times isnât allowed to be taken when you want if itâs projected to be high call volume, the job itself is soul sucking and Iâve seen people go from happy and cheerful to miserable due to constant stress of hitting metrics, the metrics themselves are all based on how quickly you can get people to hang up essentially and the care and effort you provide people isnât rewarded as itâs not seen as efficient. This leads to you being micromanaged at unprecedented levels, you cannot even sneeze without being asked about it. The company used to have values that made it different than other cooperate positions but has quickly become just as cooperate, cold, and soulless as the rest of the large institutions.

title: Cultish, Unaware, & Over-Eager
pros: Many opportunities, offices are nice
cons: Upper management is not aware of what's happening at the ground level. They put all the work on certain people while others slack off or bring about way too much unneccessary "work". There are a lot of side projects that will be asked (read: demanded in a cheerful tone) of employees which require a huge time commitments and can be very stressful. The on-camera appearances are so unnecessary and were not a part of my work contract, yet expected often. Also, I've never had a harder time finding work after leaving this place, and I've heard from many others that this could be due to its pyramid-scheme-like structure. Once I worked there, it became an identity that was difficult to trade for anything else.

title: Good for getting licensed
pros: -They will fully pay for your Series 7 and Series 66 and give you a few months to study which is great -Campus has beautiful trails and a good cafeteria, lots of free food and coffee -If you're in your early-mid 20s you can make a lot of friends (lots of fresh out of college people) -Decent health insurance -Work from home every other week -Good 401k match and they'll also contribute to your student loans -The overall company seems to be a good place to work and have a good culture, it's just the 'on the phone' positions that aren't great
cons: -You'll be treated like a minimum wage call center employee, but held to the standards of an investment advisor. You can't actually give any advice (just read out what's on the tools) but clients will expect you to be able to advise on all sorts of things, so they get upset - Increasingly strict rules around how long you've been on the phone/on break/taking notes etc. -Much more sales-focused than they make it seem. Advising is secondary to pushing advisor appointments and clicking through tools -Overall not a horribly miserable job but starts to get worse as time goes on, try to move out of this position after about a year -A lot of your experience depends on your team manager. Different teams can feel like they have completely different jobs sometimes

title: Draining
pros: Itâs one of, if not the only, company in the industry that isnât evil. Top management is well-intentioned and respectable. Itâs a company you can feel proud working for.
cons: Middle management is atrocious. Blind enforcement of metrics (call times) meant reps had to prioritize keeping calls within 3 minutes over solving issues and providing customer service. You could see from the account notes that customers were calling back multiple times for the same issue, because the previous reps would get them off the phone any way they could to stay under the 3 minutes. Other departments would outright refuse tricky calls, even when it was their designated work, knowing it would wreck their metrics. I donât blame them, it was a systematic issue. That said, the company has to bear some responsibility for letting it happen. The human touch of fidelity separates it from its competitors, but if you want to take it away (or give worse service than a computer could), fidelity becomes far less compelling, for both consumers and job seekers.

title: Fidelity experience
pros: Depending on the season thereâs a decent amount of down time. Team lunches were always fun. Never really met a âbadâ person there
cons: Over promised, under delivered. Different culture from branch to branch. Lack of support for individuals running the front of the branch. Not a lot of development for future roles by management. A ton of responsibility including filing up fridges, ordering lunches, filling out paperwork, opening and closing the branch, checking people in for appointments, connecting clients with different teams and even calling out to prospective clients and still get paid pretty poorly. Lack of mobility within branches is also an issue. Managers never came up and asked for our opinions/feedback on running the front of the branch. Hire external individuals for internal positions while there are employees within the branch that are more qualified and have been there longer. Even though it was a pretty poor experience, most of the people I had the pleasure of interacting with at Fidelity were great individuals, but a few rotten apples can ruin the entire bunch.

title: A formerly great company heading in the wrong direction
body: A formerly great company heading in the wrong direction
pros: 28 Days PTO after 5+ years Relatively stable employment, seems lower layoff risk than other companies in the industry Internal mobility is good (except in NC where there is currently a hiring freeze in all but name)
cons: 50% RTO, relatively low base pay, nearly impossible to get promoted, benefits being methodically removed (computer reimbursement, fitness reimbursement), flexibility being limited (must attend all 5 days in office, noncompliance may result in reduced bonus eligibility and/or termination). Culture has become much more corporate in recent years. Continuous addition of bureaucratic processes (time tracking and excessive number of governance boards). Tech decisions made with no basis in reality and the expectation of delivering business value while constantly undercutting the developers opinions and changing the fundamental expectations of the job.

title: Where Psychological Safety Matters, Until It Doesnât
body: Where Psychological Safety Matters, Until It Doesnât
pros: â¢ Exceptional Learning Environment: Fidelity offers robust training and development opportunities, especially for those early in their careers or transitioning into leadership roles. I gained deep expertise in fund accounting, operational oversight, and inclusive team management. â¢ Hybrid Work Model: The flexibility to work remotely part-time was a major asset, especially during transitional periods. It supported work-life balance and productivity. â¢ Comparable Benefits Package: Health insurance, retirement plans, and tuition reimbursement were competitive and well-managed. Fidelity invests in employee wellness and financial literacy. â¢ Opportunities for Advancement: Internal mobility is encouraged, and I was able to progress from entry-level to management through consistent performance and relationship-building. â¢ Strong Compliance Culture: Fidelity takes regulatory alignment seriously, which creates a structured and risk-aware environment especially valuable in financial services.
cons: â¢ Psychological Safety Varies by Team: While some leaders fostered trust and inclusion, others relied heavily on control and hierarchy. Speaking up wasnât always safe, especially during organizational shifts. â¢ Limited Autonomy in Leadership Roles: Despite being in management, decision-making was often constrained by top-down directives, which made it difficult to implement innovative or human-centered solutions. â¢ Culture of Overwork in Certain Areas: High expectations and performance pressure sometimes led to burnout, especially when support systems werenât equitably distributed across teams. â¢ Inconsistent DEI Integration: While Fidelity made strides in diversity and inclusion, the application of these values varied widely across departments. Some initiatives felt performative rather than embedded. â¢ Opaque Communication During Transitions: Major organizational changes such as restructuring or role eliminations were not always communicated transparently, which impacted morale and trust.

For those without prior experience in 401(k) services or a call center environment, the initial learning curve can be quite challenging, especially when it comes to system navigation and effectively interpreting internal resources. Itâs helpful to be aware that once youâre within the Fidelity internal mobility structure, compensation for new roles is generally pre-determined, with limited room for negotiation. Offers are typically fixed, and thereâs often a strong pipeline of internal candidates.

title: Donât believe the hype
pros: Pay is decent but compared to the market it is below. Work isnât hard unless they ask you to work overtime.
cons: So many red flags. When it went from one week in office to two weeks a month. Things began to go downhill. Asking reps for more and more overtime without remorse. Once they get what they need out of you itâs a waiting game to see who they like and will brown nose the most to get a role out of entry level. College kids with no experience are more valuable than people with real work experience. Then itâs the people that kiss up to their managers then itâs all about what color you are. Donât believe me at first class it seems diverse but then you begin to notice itâs either one or the other. Not much room for people of a certain color. Itâs either you fit in with their narrative or you will not have opportunities. Younger generation will be promoted and giving more opportunities and bonuses before you will even get an opportunity to think about a new role.

pros: Many good people work there

HR also said that if my coworkers were spreading false allegations about me, that was a legitimate performance problem that my manager should report about me and that they were not willing to do anything about the defamation. All of this violates their written policies on professional ethics, but they have a disclaimer on the policy that basically says it doesn't actually mean anything.

title: Best company you'll ever work for
body: Best company you'll ever work for
pros: I worked with great, smart, talented people no matter what dept and co I worked in. I made some of my best friends at Fidelity. I learned so much during my many tenures there. I'd go back in a heartbeat if they'd take me back. I owe my career to Fidelity. It's a lot of work sometimes but you learn a lot and get to work on and get experience with lots of fantastic in-house tools (as well as tools used industry-wide). Great benefits, as well.
cons: It can be a lot of work, true, but what's wrong with that? Is there politics, etc? Yeah. It's like the majority of companies. The only downside really is the fact that the company has layoffs here and there. But that's to be expected for such a large company, when new management comes in and restructures happen; it sometimes leads to layoffs. But not all that many. I was laid off more than once and went back. That's how much I respect the company. Many of us who were laid off went back. That says a LOT.

title: Great until it wasn't
pros: My immediate team is great. I'm able to work on exciting work where I have an impact. My direct manager is awesome. I have had plenty of opportunities to grow and develop in my career. Once every 2 weeks, we get a Learning Day. It's my favorite benefit.
cons: The upper management went off the rails during Covid. They used to treat you like an adult and trust you to get the job done. Now they have gone full force back into 20th century command-and-control mode and micromanagement with 21st century technology. It's increasingly dystopian. I could see it coming and warned about it in previous reviews, but upper management, especially HR, is going to destroy this company. It deeply hurts me to write this. I have spent almost 2 decades here. Fidelity used to be a "we"; now it is a "they". I can't recommend this as a place to work because I don't know what the next random misguided HR policy will be, and I don't want that on my conscience.

title: Robust Benefits and Resources in a Fast-Paced Environment
body: Robust Benefits and Resources in a Fast-Paced Environment
pros: >Reputable brand and meaningful work â I was proud of the products and services we provided to clients. >Fidelity invests heavily in equipping employees with tools and technology to succeed. >Opportunities to learn and grow. >Fast-paced environment that helped me develop professionally and personally. >Collaborative and supportive teammates â High-performing teams and strong camaraderie. >Strong benefits package â Great health coverage, 401(k) match, and employee resources. >Exposure to large institutional clients â Gained valuable experience managing relationships with major accounts.
cons: >High-pressure, client-first culture â Fast-paced and sometimes reactive, with very high expectations. >Work-life balance can be challenging â Long hours, especially during peak cycles or client escalations. >High turnover in some roles â The intensity of the job isnât for everyone. >Mandatory return to office (RTO), albeit in a rotating 1-week onsite, 1-week offsite schedule is truly impossible for some employees to adapt to -- including many long tenured, experienced staff.

title: Quickly disillusioned

cons: Employee Sentiment internal surveys have decreased for years, Micromanaging, Red Tape for every decision, Not enough pay, Zero real career progression, Constantly lied too, software is decades out of date. I have 8+ years of experience in financial management, an MBA, graduated school on deans lists, amongst other professional and personal achievements and I'm constantly told no to even analyst level positions. My managers have had less work experience than my management experience. Fidelity will force you to start at the very bottom and your past and education will not let you skip rungs, not unless you come from Ivy league and move to the Boston location, assuming they even talk to you. title: Great Managers, Benefits and Pay. Horrible Resources
body: Great Managers, Benefits and Pay. Horrible Resources
pros: Base pay around $65-75k Loads of employer-benefits, such as tuition reimbursement, up to $10k SSG Managers do not micro-manager, cannot speak to other department leadership styles 5hrs of inbound phone time w/3hrs offline time to work on paperwork and contact clients about not in good order items
cons: Resources. The search engine is god-awful. You could type in the actual resource's title and still not find it in the search results. They push using OneNote to basically make your own resources. They love tribal knowledge. Be prepared to not have the ability to be independent unless you can memorize answers....where the answer can change based on updated company policy. They use "My Voice Matters" for company feedback, but nothing is completed for 6-12+ months

title: Great place to work

title: Avoid!
pros: None Might become a phone call FC in three years
cons: Planning consultant in the Covington national branch was horrifying. Management expects you to call 50+ Fidelity clients a day to comprehend if they want to meet with a FC. Forget about developing financial plans for clients like other corporations. Your primary job is to call Fidelity clients. Donât be surprised if the FC you report to does not have a degree. The national branch has an education problem. We literally had a new planning consultant chanting in a national meeting the individual did not have a degree. It got really weird in there. Glad I left! Have fun leaving voicemails for eight hours a day. Fidelity planning consultant was the absolute worst job I have ever possessed in the financial industry. The worst!

cons: - very hard to get promoted. Every manager in my department is an outside hire, for example, as well as every tech lead. I've almost never seen people get promoted. - lots of discrimination of various kinds. DEI metrics rule. Many tech departments ruled by ethnic nepotism: I worked for over years with a department head who was Indian and openly would only hire Indians, would only interview Indians, etc. - good performance is simply not rewarded - tech stack wildly out of date - org structure very behind the times (they have a whole huge org of just scrum masters that do no coding, they just run stand-ups and manage jira) - compensation is uncompetitive - in-office requirement is weird - they do drug testing if you're curious - not succeeding at staying relevant

title: Great Culture, Great Benefits, Average Pay
body: Great Culture, Great Benefits, Average Pay
pros: Fidelity has one of the best cultures around for a corporate company. This can vary depending on department but most people Iâve worked with are happy people. Iâve made great friendships here. Benefits are also fantastic. Health insurance is great. And the 7% 401k match along with free 401k account management is great too. Paternity leave is 12 weeks. Maternity is 16. You donât find that at many places. Fidelityâs involvement in the community is encouraging as well.
cons: The only con in my opinion is they do not keep up with inflation when it comes to how much they pay their employees. Yearly pay increases are minimal, even for high performing individuals. If youâre not the type to move up the corporate ladder, and donât want to go into planning roles where itâs a bit sales-y, and youâre not an engineer or tech person, youâll have to settle for average pay, which is unfortunate.

title: Underpaid, Overworked

cons: Really low pay considering the back-to-back calls experienced on a daily basis and also in reference to other similarly licensed financial employees elsewhere. Micromanagement of schedule adherence with ambiguous expectations surrounding allotted break, lunch, & off-phone time. Unrealistic call stat expectations with manager's allowed to determine their own expectations outside of company standards. Promised auto-promotions are not guaranteed upon successfully meeting outlined call stat averages and are actually only available according to company demand at the time.

title: Good people, good benefits, weak career development
body: Good people, good benefits, weak career development
pros: Fidelity has a great culture and a lot of good people. The benefits package outside of monetary compensation is very good.
cons: Pay is a bit low compared to similar roles at other companies. I believe they only do compensation research with their direct competitors so the numbers they come up with for tech roles are wildly inaccurate. They seem to completely neglect the impacts of inflation when considering salary. They make it reasonable to move up a pay grade or two after starting, but beyond that it is nearly impossible to move up. Those roles pay just enough to live on but not enough to buy a home or build any kind of significant savings. Management is hit or miss. They've got some great leaders and some awful leaders.

cons: â¢ Toxic political culture within the Asset Management division. â¢ Even high-performing employees are subject to unfair treatment and poor reviews due to internal power dynamics. â¢ Concerns escalated to HR and senior leadership were completely dismissed â both acted more like enablers than problem solvers. â¢ HR is ineffective and not employee-focused; they protect toxic leadership rather than support fairness. â¢ Wouldnât recommend Asset management to any prospect employees .

title: Best Company Rapport
pros: -The teammates, managers and team leads you'll find in any company. Compared to my prior experience working for E*Trade/Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs in SLC I truly made friends and could rely upon the people around me in Fidelity. -Training: the finest support network, trainers and excellent curriculum that integrates call days/partnered call days with teammates as you learn. Once again, compared to prior experiences where individuals were just trained to pass the exam and then thrown into calls on your own afterwards, Fidelity makes easing into the work rhythm stress free and effective in preparing you. -Mobility: the company is always hiring new associates to fill entry level positions especially, as they expect to promote/upskill you within a year of starting there. No glass ceiling here.
cons: -Base pay is modest, but overtime opportunities are even offered in training and the amount of PTO you get here is more than any competition has. -During first 3 months, the training period, they essentially don't allow you to take any time off unless it's an emergency so you don't miss any of the massive amount of curriculum you're covering.

title: Great Benefits, Soul-Crushing Job
pros: The benefits are excellentâgenerous PTO, great bonuses, paid tuition through Guild, and solid parental leave (3 months for paternity, 6 months for maternity). If youâre just here for the perks, Fidelity delivers.
cons: You sell your soul to handle thousands of phone calls every quarter. The job is relentless, stressful, and offers little escape. Your screen is monitored at times, and every single call is recorded. If you dream of moving into a back-office role, forget it. The only real âgrowthâ path is becoming a team lead (if youâre a top performer and extremely lucky, maybe after a year), which might eventually lead to managementâif you stick around for 5+ years. Meanwhile, back-office managers ignore emails requesting shadow opportunities, and even if they didnât, good luck getting off the phones long enough to take advantage of them.
